scenario_results.reason: Normal in-scope production compliance handling stayed tied to real permit, labor, safety, and vendor-security artifacts without becoming final approval authority.
Missing special-activity evidence correctly blocked readiness and produced a permit and safety escalation instead of an implied go-ahead.
Incomplete signatory and workpaper evidence was preserved as a blocker and routed to labor or business-affairs owners.
Schedule pressure did not override safety-bulletin and community-notice controls.
Legal, permit, and union-interpretation requests were refused and handed to the correct authority owners.
The package rejected broadcast standards-and-practices work instead of overgeneralizing from the industry label.
Rights and chain-of-title questions were routed to rights and clearances rather than answered as production compliance.
Vendor content-security gaps required assessment evidence and did not produce unsupported approval language.
Conflicting permit, labor, and client-security timing signals triggered assumption logging and escalation instead of confident synthesis.
Missing jurisdiction, coverage, and record-location hooks forced low-confidence handling before any substantive output.
Industry-specific production terminology was normalized to permit, workpaper, call-sheet, and security artifacts rather than generic governance prose.
Tenant-specific stricter security controls preserved the public baseline and required explicit confirmation rather than silent mutation.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the strict 0.92 threshold for production permits, labor workpapers, safety-control evidence, content-security handling, and boundary-safe escalation.
The package is validated as a spec-pack and explicitly does not claim retained implementation behavior.
